primary_diagnosis.name: Seborrhoeic dermatitis of the face and scalp, acute exacerbation

differential.evidence_basis: Greasy yellow scale on erythema in a sebaceous-rich distribution (nasolabial folds, eyebrows, scalp) is the classic picture.
Facial dermatitis with burning; less likely without a new contactant, and greasy scale in sebaceous distribution favours seborrhoeic dermatitis.
A serious mimic that must be excluded; argues against by symmetry, chronic-relapsing course, greasy scale and absence of fever, unilateral spreading warmth or induration.

The patient's feared diagnosis; lupus malar rash characteristically spares the nasolabial folds and lacks greasy scale, whereas this eruption is worst IN the nasolabial folds with prominent greasy scaling.
Chronic-relapsing course, stress and cold-dry-weather trigger, greasy scale, symmetrical sebaceous distribution and heavy dandruff all converge.
Considered given scaling, but argues against without thick silvery micaceous scale, nail pitting or extensor-surface plaques.

Fungal dermatophyte infection can mimic facial scaling; typically asymmetrical with an active annular edge rather than symmetrical sebaceous greasy scale. A fungal culture would help only in atypical cases.
No fever/chills, no unilateral hot spreading painful area, no joint pain / mouth ulcers / photosensitivity — the serious mimics are made unlikely and the benign picture is confirmed clinically.
No fever, no unilateral spreading warmth or induration; symmetrical greasy-scaled eruption is not cellulitis.
Nasolabial-fold involvement with greasy scale, no systemic features (joints, ulcers, photosensitivity) — not a lupus malar rash.

differential.diagnosis: seborrhoeic dermatitis of the face and scalp (acute flare)
differential.evidence_basis: Benign, chronic-relapsing, topically manageable; the dramatic presentation and the patient's lupus fear are red herrings over a classic diagnosis. Telehealth cannot palpate to fully exclude early cellulitis, so conservative safety-netting is retained.

red_flags.description: Features of facial cellulitis — intensely hot, deep, painful skin, rapid unilateral spread over one cheek, or fever/chills
red_flags.clinical_significance: Facial cellulitis (including the periorbital/orbital risk) is a serious bacterial infection that requires urgent in-person assessment and systemic antibiotics; it is the key mimic telehealth cannot palpate to exclude.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated facial/periorbital cellulitis can progress to orbital cellulitis, cavernous sinus thrombosis or sepsis.

red_flags.description: Features suggesting systemic autoimmune disease — new joint pain, mouth ulcers, photosensitivity, or a fixed butterfly rash crossing the nasal bridge and sparing the nasolabial folds
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cutaneous/systemic lupus is the patient's feared diagnosis and a genuine not-to-miss; it needs GP assessment and serology rather than topical antifungal therapy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed systemic lupus can lead to undiagnosed renal, haematological or serositis complications.

red_flags.description: Failure to respond to appropriate topical therapy after two weeks, or steroid-induced skin thinning from over-use on the face
red_flags.clinical_significance: Non-response mandates review to reconsider the diagnosis (e.g. tinea, psoriasis, lupus) and to avoid facial steroid harm.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on the wrong diagnosis and prolonged inappropriate treatment, or facial skin atrophy / perioral dermatitis from unsupervised potent steroid use.

pathophysiology_summary: Seborrhoeic dermatitis is a chronic-relapsing inflammatory dermatosis of sebaceous-rich skin (scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds, glabella, external ears). It arises from an inflammatory host response to commensal Malassezia yeast lipid metabolites on sebum-rich skin, modulated by sebum composition, skin-barrier function and host factors. Flares are provoked by physiological stress, cold dry weather, and barrier disruption — here compounded by aggressive alkaline-soap scrubbing that stripped the acid mantle and amplified inflammation. It is benign and does not cause systemic illness; management is topical antifungal (to reduce Malassezia load) plus a short course of a mild topical corticosteroid (to settle inflammation), gentle skin care, and trigger avoidance, with intermittent maintenance therapy for the chronic course.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Visceral / framing bias from patient panic
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The dramatic, catastrophising presentation ('explosive', 'burning', 'terrified it's lupus') can pressure the clinician into over-investigation or over-triage of a benign, classic condition.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on the patient's feared diagnosis
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being pulled toward lupus or severe allergy because the patient raised them, rather than reading the classic seborrhoeic distribution and greasy scale.
Sending a textbook seborrhoeic dermatitis flare to urgent care or ED 'to be safe' — an over-triage error, not caution.
Confidently excluding facial cellulitis without the ability to palpate for warmth/induration; the correct move is conservative safety-netting for the cellulitis red flags, not remote certainty.
Recommending a potent/fluorinated topical steroid or an open-ended course on the face, risking skin atrophy, telangiectasia and perioral dermatitis.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation for deep dermal induration, warmth or fluctuance to exclude facial cellulitis
Definitive assessment of lesion depth and whether there is any early unilateral spread not yet visible
Skin scraping / fungal culture (KOH microscopy) to exclude tinea faciei in atypical cases
Serology (ANA/ENA) if systemic lupus were genuinely suspected
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the benign classic pattern and manage with topical antifungal + short mild topical steroid + gentle skin care and trigger advice; give explicit conservative safety-netting for the cellulitis and autoimmune red flags; arrange routine GP review at 14 days. Do NOT over-escalate an uncomplicated flare.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the benign diagnosis (the constellation is classic). The real risks are OVER-action (over-triage / over-investigation driven by patient panic) and, conversely, falsely reassuring away an evolving cellulitis that telehealth cannot palpate — hence red-flag safety-netting is mandatory.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Topical antifungal (e.g. ketoconazole) as first-line, with a short course of a mild topical corticosteroid for inflamed flares; gentle skin care; intermittent maintenance for the relapsing course.
